title: The Forgotten Inventors of the Lightbulb
summary: An American narrator explains how the lightbulb was invented by a long line of forgotten scientists, and how Edison's real talent was for organising other people's ideas.
body: Ask ten people who invented the lightbulb, and at least nine will say Thomas Edison. The answer is not exactly wrong, but it is far from the whole story. By the time Edison filed his famous patent in 1879, at least twenty other inventors had already produced working electric lamps. Some of them had been working on the problem for forty years. If their names were better known, the story of one of the most important inventions in history would look very different. The first working electric light was demonstrated by Humphry Davy in 1802, more than seventy years before Edison's patent. Davy used a battery and two carbon rods to produce a bright arc of light. It was too bright and too short-lived to be practical, but it showed that electricity could replace flame. Over the following decades, a long line of scientists in Britain, Russia, France, Germany, and the United States tried to make a stable, affordable bulb. A Canadian named Henry Woodward patented one in 1874 and sold the patent to Edison, who improved it. A British inventor called Joseph Swan demonstrated a working lamp in his own home in Newcastle in 1878, a full year before Edison filed his patent. Swan eventually took Edison to court, and the two companies were forced to merge in Britain rather than ruin each other in lawsuits. What did Edison actually do, then? Two things, mostly. First, he found a better filament — initially bamboo, later metal — that lasted longer than anything before it. Second, and more importantly, he built the entire system that made the lightbulb useful. A bulb is no use without wiring, generators, meters, and a power station to supply current. Edison's real genius was for industrial organisation, not for solitary discovery. He set up the first electrical utility in New York City and made the lightbulb work as part of a network rather than as a curiosity in a lab. This matters because it changes how we think about invention itself. The lone genius in a workshop is a story people enjoy, but it is rarely true. Almost every major invention has multiple parents who worked over decades, often unaware of each other. The person who gets the credit is usually the one who could organise the system around the invention, not the one who first imagined it. The lightbulb is one of the clearest cases. Davy, Swan, Woodward, and dozens of forgotten engineers all contributed. Edison's name survived because he built the network. If we remembered the others, we might understand more clearly how progress actually happens. It is not the work of a single mind. It is the slow accumulation of small breakthroughs, finally connected by someone who knew how to turn them into a working business.

annotations.meaningVi: đã nộp bằng sáng chế nổi tiếng của mình
annotations.exampleEn: Edison filed his famous patent in 1879.
Davy produced a bright arc of light between two carbon rods.
The first electric lamps were too short-lived to be useful.
Davy's experiment proved electricity could replace flame for lighting.
He tried hundreds of materials before finding a filament that lasted.

annotations.meaningVi: huỷ hoại nhau bằng các vụ kiện
annotations.exampleEn: Rather than ruin each other in lawsuits, the firms decided to merge.

annotations.meaningVi: dây dẫn, máy phát, đồng hồ đo điện
annotations.exampleEn: A bulb is no use without wiring, generators, and meters.
Edison set up the first electrical utility in New York City.

annotations.meaningVi: tổ chức công nghiệp (quản lý quy mô lớn)
annotations.exampleEn: Edison's real genius was for industrial organisation.
The lone genius in a workshop is a story people enjoy.

annotations.meaningVi: nhiều người cha (đẻ) — nhiều người sáng tạo ra
annotations.exampleEn: Almost every major invention has multiple parents.
Scientists in different countries often work unaware of each other.
The person who gets the credit is rarely the only one who deserves it.

annotations.phrase: organise the system around the invention
annotations.meaningVi: tổ chức cả hệ thống xung quanh phát minh đó
annotations.exampleEn: Credit usually goes to the one who organises the system around the invention.
Real progress is the slow accumulation of small breakthroughs.
Each small breakthrough seems unimportant in isolation.

grammarNotes.title: Past Perfect for Background Earlier in the Past
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Đoạn văn dùng thì quá khứ hoàn thành để chỉ những sự kiện xảy ra trước một mốc quá khứ khác: 'at least twenty other inventors had already produced working electric lamps', 'Some of them had been working on the problem for forty years', 'a full year before Edison filed his patent'. Cấu trúc: had + V3 (hoặc had + been + V-ing cho dạng tiếp diễn). Dùng để làm rõ trình tự thời gian trong tường thuật quá khứ.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The passage uses past perfect to mark events that occurred before another past event: 'at least twenty other inventors had already produced working electric lamps', 'Some of them had been working on the problem for forty years'. Form: had + past participle (or had + been + V-ing for continuous). Use to clarify time sequence in past narratives.

grammarNotes.title: Passive Voice for Historical Attribution
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Đoạn văn dùng dạng bị động để nhấn mạnh sự kiện thay vì người thực hiện: 'The first working electric light was demonstrated by Humphry Davy in 1802', 'the two companies were forced to merge'. Cấu trúc: be + V3. Trong văn lịch sử, bị động giúp tập trung vào điều quan trọng (phát minh, sự kiện) mà vẫn có thể nêu người thực hiện bằng 'by'.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The passage uses passive voice to emphasise events over agents: 'The first working electric light was demonstrated by Humphry Davy in 1802', 'the two companies were forced to merge'. Form: be + past participle. In historical writing, the passive lets you foreground the event while still naming the agent with 'by'.

translationVi: Hỏi mười người ai đã phát minh ra bóng đèn, ít nhất chín người sẽ nói Thomas Edison. Câu trả lời không hẳn sai, nhưng còn xa mới là toàn bộ câu chuyện. Đến khi Edison nộp bằng sáng chế nổi tiếng năm 1879, ít nhất hai mươi nhà phát minh khác đã sản xuất được đèn điện hoạt động. Một số đã làm việc với vấn đề này trong bốn mươi năm. Nếu tên họ được biết đến nhiều hơn, câu chuyện về một trong những phát minh quan trọng nhất lịch sử sẽ trông rất khác. Ánh sáng điện hoạt động đầu tiên được Humphry Davy trình diễn năm 1802, hơn bảy mươi năm trước bằng sáng chế của Edison. Davy dùng pin và hai thanh than để tạo ra một cung sáng rực rỡ. Nó quá sáng và quá ngắn ngủi để thực dụng, nhưng đã cho thấy điện có thể thay thế ngọn lửa. Trong các thập kỷ tiếp theo, một loạt nhà khoa học ở Anh, Nga, Pháp, Đức và Mỹ cố gắng làm ra bóng đèn ổn định, giá phải chăng. Một người Canada tên Henry Woodward đã được cấp bằng sáng chế năm 1874 và bán bằng cho Edison, người đã cải tiến nó. Một nhà phát minh người Anh tên Joseph Swan đã trình diễn đèn hoạt động tại nhà ở Newcastle năm 1878, đầy một năm trước khi Edison nộp bằng. Swan cuối cùng đã kiện Edison ra toà, và hai công ty buộc phải sáp nhập ở Anh thay vì huỷ hoại nhau bằng kiện cáo. Vậy Edison thực sự đã làm gì? Hai điều chủ yếu. Thứ nhất, ông tìm được sợi tóc tốt hơn — ban đầu là tre, sau là kim loại — bền hơn bất kỳ thứ gì trước đó. Thứ hai, và quan trọng hơn, ông xây dựng cả hệ thống làm cho bóng đèn trở nên hữu dụng. Bóng đèn vô dụng nếu không có dây dẫn, máy phát, đồng hồ đo, và nhà máy điện để cung cấp dòng. Thiên tài thực sự của Edison là tổ chức công nghiệp, không phải khám phá đơn độc. Ông thành lập công ty điện đầu tiên ở thành phố New York và làm cho bóng đèn hoạt động như một phần của mạng lưới chứ không phải vật lạ trong phòng thí nghiệm. Điều này quan trọng vì nó thay đổi cách ta nghĩ về phát minh. Thiên tài đơn độc trong xưởng là câu chuyện người ta thích, nhưng hiếm khi đúng. Hầu hết mọi phát minh lớn đều có nhiều người cha đẻ, làm việc qua nhiều thập kỷ, thường không biết về nhau. Người được ghi công thường là người có thể tổ chức cả hệ thống xung quanh phát minh, không phải người đầu tiên hình dung ra nó. Bóng đèn là một trong những trường hợp rõ ràng nhất. Davy, Swan, Woodward, và hàng chục kỹ sư bị quên lãng đều đã đóng góp. Tên Edison còn lại vì ông đã xây dựng mạng lưới. Nếu ta nhớ những người khác, ta có thể hiểu rõ hơn cách tiến bộ thực sự xảy ra. Nó không phải công việc của một bộ óc duy nhất. Nó là sự tích luỹ chậm chạp của những đột phá nhỏ, cuối cùng được kết nối bởi ai đó biết cách biến chúng thành một doanh nghiệp hoạt động.

questions.prompt: When did Edison file his famous patent?
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'Edison filed his famous patent in 1879.'

questions.prompt: How many other inventors had already produced working electric lamps by the time Edison filed?
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'at least twenty other inventors had already produced working electric lamps.'
questions.hint: A specific minimum number is named.

questions.prompt: Who demonstrated the first working electric light, and when?
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'The first working electric light was demonstrated by Humphry Davy in 1802.'
questions.hint: Listen for the earliest date in the passage.

options: A battery and two carbon rods
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'Davy used a battery and two carbon rods to produce a bright arc of light.'

questions.prompt: Why was Davy's light not practical?
options: It was too expensive to produce
It was too bright and too short-lived
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'It was too bright and too short-lived to be practical.'

questions.prompt: Who patented a bulb in 1874 and sold the patent to Edison?
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'A Canadian named Henry Woodward patented one in 1874 and sold the patent to Edison.'
questions.hint: Name and nationality are both given.

questions.prompt: What did Joseph Swan do in Newcastle in 1878?
options: He demonstrated a working lamp in his own home
He founded the first electrical utility
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'Joseph Swan demonstrated a working lamp in his own home in Newcastle in 1878.'
questions.hint: An act of demonstration is described.

questions.prompt: How was the Edison-Swan dispute resolved in Britain?
options: Swan retired and gave up his claim
The two companies were forced to merge
The court ruled in Edison's favour
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'the two companies were forced to merge in Britain rather than ruin each other in lawsuits.'
questions.hint: The outcome was a corporate merger.

questions.prompt: What two things did Edison actually contribute?
options: Inventing the wire and inventing the switch
A better filament and the entire electrical supply system
The vacuum bulb and the glass casing
Patenting the design and writing the manual
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'First, he found a better filament... Second, and more importantly, he built the entire system.'
questions.hint: Two contributions are listed — one technical, one systemic.

questions.prompt: What did Edison set up in New York City?
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'He set up the first electrical utility in New York City.'

questions.prompt: What does the narrator say is rarely true about invention?
options: It is the work of a lone genius in a workshop
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'The lone genius in a workshop is a story people enjoy, but it is rarely true.'
questions.hint: The narrator challenges a romantic myth.

questions.prompt: According to the narrator, who usually gets the credit for an invention?
options: The person who first imagined it
The person who could organise the system around the invention
The person who patented it first
The person who wrote about it first
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'The person who gets the credit is usually the one who could organise the system around the invention.'
questions.hint: Credit goes to organisation, not imagination.

questions.prompt: What overall thesis does the passage advance about progress?
options: Progress is the work of singular geniuses we should celebrate
Progress is the slow accumulation of small breakthroughs finally connected by someone who can turn them into a working business
Progress is impossible without government funding
questions.explanation: The narrator concludes 'It is the slow accumulation of small breakthroughs, finally connected by someone who knew how to turn them into a working business.'
questions.hint: Look at the final paragraph's definition of progress.

cloze.template: Ask ten people who invented the lightbulb, and at least nine will say Thomas Edison. The answer is not exactly wrong, but it is far from the whole story. By the time Edison filed his famous {{b1}} in 1879, at least twenty other inventors had already produced working electric lamps. The first working electric light was demonstrated by Humphry Davy in 1802. Davy used a battery and two carbon rods to produce a bright {{b2}} of light. It was too short-lived to be practical, but it showed that electricity could replace {{b3}}. A Canadian named Henry Woodward patented one in 1874 and sold the patent to Edison. A British inventor called Joseph Swan demonstrated a working lamp in his own home in Newcastle in 1878. Swan eventually took Edison to court, and the two companies were forced to {{b4}} in Britain rather than ruin each other in lawsuits. What did Edison actually do? First, he found a better {{b5}} — initially bamboo, later metal. Second, he built the entire system that made the lightbulb useful. A bulb is no use without wiring, generators, meters, and a power station. Edison's real {{b6}} was for industrial organisation, not solitary discovery. He set up the first electrical {{b7}} in New York City. The {{b8}} genius in a workshop is a story people enjoy, but it is rarely true. The person who gets the {{b9}} is usually the one who could organise the system around the invention. Progress is the slow {{b10}} of small breakthroughs.

blanks.explanation: 'Patent' is the exact legal term used — the legal right to an invention.
'Arc of light' is the technical term — light formed between two electrodes.
The exact word the narrator uses — 'replace flame' is the historical contrast with electric light.
'Merge' is the exact verb — meaning two companies became one corporate entity.
'Filament' is the specific technical term for the wire inside a bulb that glows.
The narrator uses 'genius' specifically — to contrast with the lone-genius myth they're challenging.
'Electrical utility' is the exact phrase — the standard term for a power company.
'Lone genius' is the exact mythic phrase the narrator is critiquing.
'Gets the credit' is the fixed phrase — to be recognised as the originator.
'Slow accumulation' is the exact phrase used — gradual addition over time.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The narrator argues that credit for an invention usually goes to whoever organises the system around it, not whoever imagines it first. Is this fair, or do we owe more recognition to the original thinkers — and how would more accurate credit change the way young people think about their own work?
